message:
📖 docs(ladder): shift level criteria from PR counts to leaderboard points (#1500)

Contributor (Khushal Malhotra, Slack 2026-04-20) pointed out the Ladder
page still listed the legacy PR-count criteria ('Merge at least 20 PRs
(8 within first 6 weeks)', 'Open at least 6 help wanted issues') even
though the program now counts leaderboard points. Updating the ladder
page to match.

### Changes

1. `messages/en.json` — `ladderPage.levels.*.requirements` rewritten in
   terms of leaderboard points:
     - Contributor: 'Earn ≥500 points'
     - Unpaid Mentee: '≥10,000 points in 12 weeks, ≥4,000 in first 6 weeks'
     - Paid Mentee: 'Sustain ≥3,000 points/month'
     - Mentor: qualitative + 'consistent point growth'
     - Maintainer: '≥2,000 points every 2 months' + reviews + meetings
     - Ambassador: 'maintainer-level leaderboard activity'

2. `messages/en.json` — new `pointsNote` and
   `pointsNoteCtaLeaderboard` keys. The note explains the shift and
   lists the leaderboard point values (500/300/200/100/50) inline so
   readers don't need to leave the page to interpret the thresholds.

3. `src/app/[locale]/ladder/page.tsx` — render the new `pointsNote`
   below the existing subtitle with a link to /leaderboard.

4. `messages/{de,es,it,ja,zh-CN}.json` — strip stale translated copies
   of `levels.*.requirements` and `levels.*.goodStanding` so those
   locales fall back to the updated English instead of rendering the
   outdated PR-count translations. Titles, descriptions, nextLevel and
   timeframe strings stay in-language (those weren't semantic drift).

### Why these thresholds

Roughly calibrated to the legacy counts:

| Legacy | Points equivalent |
|---|---|
| 20 merged PRs | 20 × 500 = 10,000 |
| 8 merged PRs within 6 weeks | 8 × 500 = 4,000 |
| 3 PRs + 2 issues every 2 months | 3 × 500 + 2 × ~175 ≈ 2,000 |
| Starter contribution (~1 PR) | 500 |

Future locale translations can retranslate the new English text via
the normal translation workflow. Not included in this PR to limit
scope — the stale translations were strictly worse than English
fallback.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Anderson <andy@clubanderson.com>
